description:
You are the backup administrator for a small team.  
    A project report directory already exists at   

        /home/user/reports

    Your task is to create a compressed tar archive of that directory and generate
    a human-readable log of the archive’s contents – all in one, single terminal
    command.

    Required end-state of the system
    --------------------------------
    1. Directory  
          /home/user/backups  
       must exist.  (Create it if it is missing.)

    2. A gzip-compressed tar file named  
          /home/user/backups/reports_backup.tar.gz  
       must be produced.  It must contain the entire **reports** directory tree,
       preserving paths **relative to the root filesystem** exactly as tar shows
       them when called with the “v” (verbose) option.  In other words, if you
       list the archive verbosely you should see lines such as  
          home/user/reports/report1.txt  
       and so on (see the exact list below).

    3. A plain-text log file named  
          /home/user/backups/backup_log.txt  
       must be created **by the same single command that creates the archive**.
       The log must contain nothing except the standard verbose listing produced
       by GNU tar when the archive is created.  Each entry must occupy exactly
       one line, with the classical tar “ls -like” fields (permissions, owner,
       group, size, date, time, pathname).  The automated grader will perform a
       strict, line-by-line comparison, so avoid any extra output.

       The log must show the following five lines, in this exact order:

           drwxr-xr-x ... home/user/reports/
           -rw-r--r-- ... home/user/reports/report1.txt
           -rw-r--r-- ... home/user/reports/report2.txt
           drwxr-xr-x ... home/user/reports/summary/
           -rw-r--r-- ... home/user/reports/summary/quarter3.txt

       (“…” stands for the date, time, owner, group, and size fields generated
       automatically by tar; do not attempt to edit or re-format them.)

    4. The contents of the archive must exactly reproduce the original directory
       tree when extracted.

    Hints (do NOT echo the commands here):
    • GNU tar can write both the archive and its verbose listing simultaneously.  
    • Redirection with “>” or “tee” is allowed, but everything must be combined
      into a single shell command issued once.

    When you are finished, the grader will:
       a) Confirm that /home/user/backups/reports_backup.tar.gz exists and is a
          valid gzip-compressed tar file.
       b) Extract it into a temporary directory and verify that the directory
          hierarchy and file names match exactly.
       c) Read /home/user/backups/backup_log.txt and compare its five lines
          against the expected path list shown above.

    Perform the steps in a Linux terminal.  Remember: only one terminal command
    should accomplish both the archive creation and the log creation.
truth:
Pre-existing items before the task starts
    ----------------------------------------
    1. Directory /home/user/reports with the following structure:
       /home/user/reports/
           report1.txt      (text: "Q1 financial summary\n")
           report2.txt      (text: "Q2 financial summary\n")
           summary/
               quarter3.txt (text: "Q3 details\n")

    2. No /home/user/backups directory yet.  The agent must create it.

    Verification specifics
    ----------------------
    • The archive must be exactly at /home/user/backups/reports_backup.tar.gz

    • The log file must be exactly at /home/user/backups/backup_log.txt

    • The grader will run:
          tar -tzf /home/user/backups/reports_backup.tar.gz
      and expect the following 5 pathnames in this order:

          home/user/reports/
          home/user/reports/report1.txt
          home/user/reports/report2.txt
          home/user/reports/summary/
          home/user/reports/summary/quarter3.txt

    • Then it will read backup_log.txt and check that it contains exactly 5
      lines whose last field matches the list above, in the same order.

    • The grader tolerates any timestamp, size, owner and group values produced
      by tar, but the pathnames and ordering must be exact.

    • Permissions of the created files/directories are not strictly graded, but
      normal user defaults are expected.
